description: Self-contained Knowlex RAG evaluation set. v4 (ADR-0049 § 7th arc, 2026-04-26): introduces `expectedSubstringsAny` OR-mode scoring alongside the strict `expectedSubstrings` AND mode, so paraphrase-tolerant questions accept Gemini's natural-language variants ('free tier' OR 'free-tier', 'Singapore' OR 'Singapore region', 'ring buffer' OR 'memory buffer'). Run 6 (post temp 0.7 + safety BLOCK_NONE under v3 substring-AND) measured 4/30 = 13.3% pass with 26 paraphrase failures retrieving correct citations. v4 separates retrieval-correctness from paraphrase-tolerance so the metric measures what it claims to measure. Thresholds at v0.5.1: minPassRate 0.6, maxP95LatencyMs 10000.

corpus.title: Knowlex RAG architecture
corpus.content: Knowlex is a retrieval-augmented generation app in the craftstack monorepo. Documents pasted into the /kb page are split into paragraph-aware 512-character chunks, embedded with the gemini-embedding-001 model at 768 dimensions via the outputDimensionality provider option, and stored in a pgvector column on Neon Postgres. Retrieval uses cosine distance (the <=> operator) over an HNSW index, returning the top-K nearest chunks for the query, which are then cited by number in the streamed Gemini 2.0 Flash answer.

corpus.title: Boardly realtime architecture
corpus.content: Boardly is a realtime collaborative kanban app. Card positions use LexoRank strings for O(1) reordering — moving a card between two neighbours only writes one row. Every mutation broadcasts via Pusher Channels to all clients currently watching the board. Card updates use optimistic locking with a version column; stale writes return HTTP 409 VERSION_MISMATCH and the client replays after refreshing its local state.

corpus.title: Security posture
corpus.content: The craftstack monorepo scores an A grade on securityheaders.com. The Content Security Policy allowlists Vercel platform scripts with unsafe-inline because an earlier nonce-based CSP with strict-dynamic broke hydration on Vercel's runtime (platform-injected scripts did not carry the per-request nonce). Every other hardened header remains intact: HSTS with a two-year preload, X-Frame-Options DENY, Cross-Origin-Opener-Policy same-origin, and a Permissions-Policy denying seventeen unused sensor and media capabilities. See ADR-0040 for the full rollback rationale.

corpus.title: Cost safety regime
corpus.content: craftstack enforces a zero-dollar monthly infrastructure bill through three complementary mechanisms introduced in ADR-0046. A PR-blocking free-tier-compliance CI gate (scripts/check-free-tier-compliance.mjs) blocks any commit that would introduce a paid Vercel plan, a billable-only SDK like stripe or @vercel/kv, or a real-looking secret pattern in .env.example. An EMERGENCY_STOP=1 environment flag short-circuits the three Gemini-consuming routes (/api/kb/ask and /api/kb/ingest on knowledge, /api/kb/ask on collab playground) and returns HTTP 503 with Retry-After 3600. A /api/kb/budget observability endpoint exposes the current day/month usage against caps, gated behind ENABLE_OBSERVABILITY_API=1 in production because used-vs-cap ratios are tactical attack intelligence. The STRIDE threat model gains a new Cost exhaustion category with rows C-01 through C-06 covering single-IP flood, IP rotation, Gemini key leak, silent infra upgrade, slow operator response, and oversize ingest.

corpus.title: Undo redo and optimistic locking
corpus.content: Boardly's card move undo/redo stack is a 25-entry client-only LIFO recorded in move-history.ts, introduced in ADR-0036. Each entry stores cardId, from, and to endpoints; Ctrl-Z replays the move against the live /api/cards/:id/move endpoint with the stored version. ADR-0048 tightens the interaction contract with broadcast events: when a Pusher card.moved or card.deleted event arrives for a card whose id appears in the local undo or redo stack, the matching entries are marked stale before the local view updates. Pressing Ctrl-Z on a stale entry shows a toast reading 'Your last move was modified by another user. Skipping to the previous undo-able action.' and continues popping until a non-stale entry is found. card.updated events that change title, labels, or assignees do NOT mark entries stale — undo is scoped to moves only, matching the user mental model of 'undo my last drag.'

corpus.title: Workspace tenancy and four-tier RBAC
corpus.content: Boardly is multi-tenant on workspaces. ADR-0023 defines a four-tier role hierarchy — OWNER, ADMIN, EDITOR, VIEWER — with a roleAtLeast comparator used as a gate on every workspace-scoped mutation. ADR-0029 adds cross-workspace guards on set-mutations: applying a label, assigning a member, or moving a card to a list all validate that both source and target belong to the same workspace before the write succeeds, preventing cross-tenant data mixing even when a buggy or malicious client supplies a foreign id. Knowlex inherits the same four-tier model in ADR-0047 when workspace tenancy ships; retrieval queries gain a WHERE document.workspaceId = :id clause that pre-filters vector search results before the HNSW kNN runs.

corpus.title: LexoRank card ordering
corpus.content: Card and list ordering uses LexoRank strings via the lexorank npm package (ADR-0006 and ADR-0025). A LexoRank is a short, base-36-adjacent string that sits between any two others — inserting a card between 'U' and 'V' computes a rank like 'Uh' without rewriting either neighbour. This makes drag-and-drop reorders O(1) in rows written: each drag updates exactly one row's rank field. The alternative — storing cards in an integer position column — would require renumbering every card after the insert point, which is O(n) in rows written and scales badly past a hundred cards per list.

corpus.title: Token-hashed invitation system
corpus.content: Workspace invitations in Boardly use the token-hashed pattern from ADR-0026. The server generates a high-entropy random token and stores only its SHA-256 hash in the invitation row; the plaintext token is emitted once in the acceptUrl and never persisted. Accepting an invitation hashes the presented token and matches against the stored hash — rainbow-table attacks on a compromised database cannot recover usable invitation tokens. Each token is bound to the invitee's email so that interception and replay by a third party fails the email match. ADR-0027 layers three rate limits on top: per-invitee (five invitations per day per email), per-inviter (ten per hour), and per-workspace (fifty per day), preventing a single compromised admin from blasting the entire Resend free-tier quota.

corpus.title: Deployment topology
corpus.content: craftstack ships to Vercel Hobby as two separate projects: craftstack-collab hosts Boardly plus the Knowlex playground, while craftstack-knowledge hosts the standalone Knowlex RAG surface. Both projects connect to Neon Postgres in Singapore on the free tier. Collab uses Upstash Redis in Tokyo for rate limiter state and presence cache; Pusher Channels handles realtime fanout; Resend sends invitation emails. Knowledge runs only against its own Neon database with the pgvector extension enabled. All external services have free tiers with no credit card required; the check-free-tier-compliance CI gate blocks any commit that would move this off free tier. See docs/FREE_TIER_ONBOARDING.md for step-by-step signup.

corpus.title: Observability pipeline
corpus.content: Every captureException call in both apps flows through a DSN-gated helper in src/lib/observability.ts that forwards to Sentry when SENTRY_DSN is configured and otherwise stashes the event into a per-container in-memory ring buffer. ADR-0044 and ADR-0045 document the full pipeline. The /api/observability/captures endpoint dumps the ring buffer as JSON and is open in dev and preview but closed in production unless ENABLE_OBSERVABILITY_API=1. error.tsx global boundaries on both apps route through this seam. Knowlex adds /api/kb/stats (integrity probe returning storedDim, expectedDim, orphanEmbeddings, indexType) and /api/kb/budget (per-container usage snapshot) as additional observability endpoints. A 6-hourly Live smoke workflow in .github/workflows/smoke.yml hits these endpoints on the production URLs to detect silent regressions.
